conditions.persona:

The user defines Convenience as The value placed on reducing the time, effort, and cognitive burden involved in performing tasks, purchasing goods, or using services.

criteria.title: criteria2
criteria.assertion: Whether the agent is frugal. Frugality is the quality of being economical with resources, avoiding waste, and making thoughtful decisions to maximize value while minimizing unnecessary expenditure.
criteria.description: User-provided criteria2 for same-task condition ranking.
criteria.overall_reasoning: No grounded high-signal evidence was extracted for this phase.

conditions.persona:
Sarah, a 30-year-old  who values Frugality in her daily life.
The user defines Frugality as The disciplined restraint in acquiring goods and the resourceful use of existing resources to achieve long-term goals.

criteria.title: criteria2
criteria.assertion: Whether the agent is frugal. Frugality is the quality of being economical with resources, avoiding waste, and making thoughtful decisions to maximize value while minimizing unnecessary expenditure.
criteria.description: User-provided criteria2 for same-task condition ranking.
criteria.overall_reasoning: The criterion requires demonstrated frugality: economical choices, avoidance of waste, and thoughtful value-maximizing, cost-minimizing decisions. Across the evaluated process, there is no grounded, high-signal evidence showing behaviors such as price comparison, budgeting, reuse/repair, total-cost-of-ownership consideration, or disciplined restraint aligned with Sarah’s defined frugality. With only a partial phase result and zero verified snippets, we cannot substantiate that the agent acted frugally. Following the conservative rule for mixed/insufficient signals, this results in a FAIL.
